subject: Physics
q: Two identical thin biconvex lenses of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are in contact with each other. The space between the lenses is filled with a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>. The focal length of the combination is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Two identical thin biconvex lenses of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are in contact with each other. The space between the lenses is filled with a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math>. The focal length of the combination is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
For convex lenses, let radius of curvature is R. Then,

115 cm=1.5-11R-1-R

⇒1R=115 ⇒R=15 cm

Now for concave lens,

1fconcave=1.25-11-R-1R

=14×-2R=-130

1feff=115+115-130=-110

⇒feff=-10 cm

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of a prism with apex angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The angle of minimum deviation is :
question: The refractive index of a prism with apex angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The angle of minimum deviation is :
solution:
As we know, μ=sinA+δm2sinA2

⇒cosA2sinA2=sinA+δm2sinA2

⇒sinπ2-A2=sinA+δm2

⇒π2-A2=A2+δm2

⇒δm=π-2A=180°-2A

subject: Physics
q: A transparent solid cylindrical rod has a refractive index of $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$. It is surrounded by air. A light ray is incident at the mid point of one end of the rod as shown in the figure. The incident angle $\theta$ for which the light ray grazes along the wall of the rod is
question: A transparent solid cylindrical rod has a refractive index of $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$. It is surrounded by air. A light ray is incident at the mid point of one end of the rod as shown in the figure. The incident angle $\theta$ for which the light ray grazes along the wall of the rod is
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \sin c=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \sin r=\sin (90-c)=\cos c=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \frac{\sin \theta}{\sin r}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} \\ & \sin \theta=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & \theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: A light ray is incident on a glass slab of thickness $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ . The angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle for the glass slab with air. The lateral displacement of ray after passing through glass slab is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. $\left(\right.$ Given $\left.\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25\right)$
question: A light ray is incident on a glass slab of thickness $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ . The angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle for the glass slab with air. The lateral displacement of ray after passing through glass slab is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. $\left(\right.$ Given $\left.\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25\right)$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & i=\theta_c \\ & \Rightarrow i=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\mu}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow i=45^{\circ} \end{aligned}$ and according to snell's law $\begin{aligned} & 1 \sin 45^{\circ}=\sqrt{2} \sin \mathrm{r} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=30^{\circ} \end{aligned}$
Lateral displacement $\Delta=\frac{\mathrm{t} \sin (\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{r})}{\cos \mathrm{r}}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \Delta=\frac{4 \sqrt{3} \times \sin 15^{\circ}}{\cos 30^{\circ}} \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta=2 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Angle of minimum deviation is equal to the half of the angle of prism in an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is __________.
question: Angle of minimum deviation is equal to the half of the angle of prism in an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is __________.
solution:
For an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 60^{\circ}$.

Given that the angle of minimum deviation is half of the angle of the prism:

$\delta_{m} = \dfrac{A}{2} = \dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2} = 30^{\circ}$

The refractive index of the prism is given by:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{A + \delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$

Substituting the values of $A$ and $\delta_{m}$:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ} + 30^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}$

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin(45^{\circ})}{\sin(30^{\circ})}$

$\mu = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \sqrt{2}$

Answer: $\sqrt{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
question: A monochromatic light wave with wavelength λ1 and frequency ν1 in air enters another medium. If the angle of incidence and angle of refraction at the interface are 45° and 30° respectively, then the wavelength λ2 and frequency ν2 of the refracted wave are:
solution:
By using Snell's law, n1sinθ1=n2sinθ2, the refractive index is 

μ=sin45°sin30°=1212=2

Also using the relation n=cv where n is the refractive index, c is the velocity of light in air and v is the velocity in the medium

⇒cair v =2=λ1 λ2

The velocities are given by 

v=νλ2c=νλ1

⇒λ1=2λ2 and ν1=ν2.

The frequency of light does not change when the medium changes.

subject: Physics
q: A biconvex lens of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in air. Its focal length when immersed in a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn></math> will be:
question: A biconvex lens of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in air. Its focal length when immersed in a liquid of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn></math> will be:
solution:
Given, the refractive index of the material of the lens is μ1=1.5, the refractive index of the medium is μm=1.6 and the focal length of the lens in air is fa=20 cm.

In air:

1fa=μ1-12R

In liquid:

1fm=μ1μm-12R

The focal length as measured in air and that as measured inside the liquid medium are related by, 

fmfa=μ1-1μmμ1-μm   ...1

Equation (1) implies that

fm20=(1.5-1)1.6(1.5-1.6)⇒fm=-160 cm

subject: Physics
q: The following figure represents two biconvex lenses $L_1$ and $L_2$ having focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The distance between $L_1 \& L_2$ is :
question: The following figure represents two biconvex lenses $L_1$ and $L_2$ having focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The distance between $L_1 \& L_2$ is :
solution: $D=f_1+f_2=25 \mathrm{~cm}$ Paraxial parallel rays pass through focus and ray from focus of convex lens will become parallel

subject: Physics
q: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
question: In the diagram given below, there are three lenses formed. Considering negligible thickness of each of them as compared to $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$, i.e., the radii of curvature for upper and lower surfaces of the glass lens, the power of the combination is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow p_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{p}_1+\mathrm{p}_2+\mathrm{p}_3 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{4}{3}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_1=\left(\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_3=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=-\frac{1}{3\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right) \\ & =-\frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_1\right|}-\frac{1}{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A vessel of depth d is half filled with oil of refractive index n1 and the other half is filled with water of refractive index n2. The apparent depth of this vessel when viewed from above will be-
question: A vessel of depth d is half filled with oil of refractive index n1 and the other half is filled with water of refractive index n2. The apparent depth of this vessel when viewed from above will be-
solution:
The apparent depth formula is given by n=Real depthApparent depth.

The apparent depth in this case is given by 

d'=d1n1+d2n2⇒d'=d2(1n1+1n2)⇒d'=d2(n1+n2n1n2)

subject: Physics
q: A telescope has an objective lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and an eyepiece of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall tower at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> is observed through this telescope in a normal setting, the angle formed by the image of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> is close to
question: A telescope has an objective lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and an eyepiece of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall tower at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> is observed through this telescope in a normal setting, the angle formed by the image of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> is close to
solution:
Angular magnification of a telescope in normal adjustment is

m=βα=f0fe⇒β=f0feα



According to the question

α=yx=501000=120

⇒α=120

⇒β=1505×120=32 rad

=32×180°3.14=86°

subject: Physics
q: Light ray incident along a vector $\vec{AO}$ $(\vec{AO} = 2\hat{i}-3\hat{j})$ emerges out along vector $\vec{OB}$ $(\vec{OB} = C\hat{i}-4\hat{j})$ as shown in the figure below. The value of $C$ is ________.
question: Light ray incident along a vector $\vec{AO}$ $(\vec{AO} = 2\hat{i}-3\hat{j})$ emerges out along vector $\vec{OB}$ $(\vec{OB} = C\hat{i}-4\hat{j})$ as shown in the figure below. The value of $C$ is ________.
solution:
From the given figure, the interface is horizontal and the normal is along the y-axis.

The incident ray is along the vector $\vec{AO} = 2\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}$.
The angle of incidence $\alpha$ is the angle between the incident ray and the normal.
$\sin \alpha = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$

The refracted ray is along the vector $\vec{OB} = C\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}$.
The angle of refraction $\beta$ is the angle between the refracted ray and the normal.
$\sin \beta = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + (-4)^2}} = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$

Applying Snell's law at the interface:
$\mu_1 \sin \alpha = \mu_2 \sin \beta$
$1 \times \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$
$\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$

Squaring both sides:
$\dfrac{4}{13} = \dfrac{9}{4} \left( \dfrac{C^2}{C^2 + 16} \right)$
$16(C^2 + 16) = 117 C^2$
$16 C^2 + 256 = 117 C^2$
$101 C^2 = 256$
$C^2 = \dfrac{256}{101}$
$C = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{101}} \approx 1.592$

Approximating $\sqrt{101} \approx 10$, we get $C \approx 1.6$.

Answer: $1.6$

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light is incident from air on a glass plate having thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of incidence of a ray is equal to the critical angle for glass-air interface. The lateral displacement of the ray when it passes through the plate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><msup><mn>10</mn><mo>-</mo></msup><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>cm</mi></math> . (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math>)
question: A ray of light is incident from air on a glass plate having thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math>. The angle of incidence of a ray is equal to the critical angle for glass-air interface. The lateral displacement of the ray when it passes through the plate is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><msup><mn>10</mn><mo>-</mo></msup><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>cm</mi></math> . (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math>)
solution:
Let C be the critical angle.

Then, sinC=1μ, where, μ is the refractive index of glass.

So, sinC=12⇒C=45°

As the angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle, so using Snell's law,

 sinC=μsinθ

⇒12=2 sinθ

⇒θ=30°

Now, lateral displacement of the ray is 

x=t sin(i-r) sec r

⇒x=3sin 45°-30°sec 30°

⇒x=3(0.26)23

⇒x=0.52 cm

⇒x=52×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q: An object $2.4 \mathrm{~m}$ in front of a lens forms a sharp image on a film $12 \mathrm{~cm}$ behind the lens. A glass plate $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick, of refractive index $1.50$ is interposed between lens and film with its plane faces parallel to film. At what distance (from lens) should object be shifted to be in sharp focus on film?
question: An object $2.4 \mathrm{~m}$ in front of a lens forms a sharp image on a film $12 \mathrm{~cm}$ behind the lens. A glass plate $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick, of refractive index $1.50$ is interposed between lens and film with its plane faces parallel to film. At what distance (from lens) should object be shifted to be in sharp focus on film?
solution:
Case $\mathrm{I}: \mathrm{u}=-240 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{v}=12$, by Lens formula
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{7}{80}$
Case $\mathrm{II}$: $\mathrm{v}=12-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{35}{3}$ (normal shift $=1-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{3}$ )
$f=\frac{7}{80}$
$u=5.6$

subject: Physics
q: The variation of refractive index of a crown glass thin prism with wavelength of the incident light is shown. Which of the following graphs is the correct one, if $D_{m}$ is the angle of minimum deviation?
question: The variation of refractive index of a crown glass thin prism with wavelength of the incident light is shown. Which of the following graphs is the correct one, if $D_{m}$ is the angle of minimum deviation?
solution:
When angle of prism is small, then angle of deviation

is given by $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{m}}=(\mu-1) \mathrm{A}$

So, if wavelength of incident light is increased, $\mu$ decreases

and hence $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{m}}$ decreases.

subject: Physics
q: Two convex lenses of focal length 20 cm each are placed coaxially with a separation of 60 cm between them. The image of the distant object formed by the combination is at _____ cm from the first lens.
question: Two convex lenses of focal length 20 cm each are placed coaxially with a separation of 60 cm between them. The image of the distant object formed by the combination is at _____ cm from the first lens.
solution:
Let L1 be the first lens and L2 be the second lens.

The first refraction is in L1.

Using lens formula,

u1=∞, f1=20 cm,1v1-0=1f1 ⇒v1=20 cm

Hence, the distance between the second image and the L2 would be 60-20=40 cm.

For the second lens L2,

u2=-40 cm, f2=20 cm,1v2-1u2=1f2⇒1v2+140=120⇒1v2=140⇒v2=40 cm

So, the distance of image from first lens is

 x=40 cm+60 cm

=100 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two objects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole in front of a concave mirror having radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The distance between images formed by the mirror is:
question: Two objects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole in front of a concave mirror having radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The distance between images formed by the mirror is:
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram-

Let's assume that vA, vB indicates the distances of the images of objects A and B respectively.

Thus, by mirror formula, for object A, it can be written that

1v+1u=1f1vA+1-15=1-201vA=1-20+115=-3+460vA=60 cm

Similarly, for object B, using mirror formula,

1vB+1-25=1-201vB=-120+125=-5+4100=-1100vB=-100 cm

Hence, the distance d between the images can be calculated as follows:

d= vA+vB= 60+100 cm= 160 cm

subject: Physics
q: A diver looking up through the water sees the outside world contained in a circular horizon. The refractive index of water is <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, and the diver's eyes are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> below the surface of the water. Then the radius of the circle is :
question: A diver looking up through the water sees the outside world contained in a circular horizon. The refractive index of water is <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, and the diver's eyes are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> below the surface of the water. Then the radius of the circle is :
solution:
∴    sin  θ c = 1 μ

                = 3 4

∴    r h 2 + r 2 = 3 4

∴    r 2 h 2 + r 2 = 9 1 6

∴    1 6 r 2 = 9 h 2 + 9 r 2

          7 r 2 = 9 h 2

             r = h 9 7

               = 3 h 7

               = 3 7 × 1 5  cm

question:

subject: Physics
q: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
question: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
solution:
The speed of light in a medium is inversely proportional to the refractive index of the material. Therefore,

μAμB=vBvA=12

Let the thickness is d

⇒t2-t1=dvB-dvA=5×10-10 s

As vA=2vB⇒d=5×10-10×2vB

⇒d=5×10-10×vA m.

q: Light from a point source in air falls on a convex curved surface of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. If the source is located at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the convex surface, the image will be formed at ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> from the object.
question: Light from a point source in air falls on a convex curved surface of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. If the source is located at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the convex surface, the image will be formed at ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> from the object.
solution:
μ2v-μ1u=μ2−μ1R

⇒1.5v−1−100=1.5−120

⇒v=100 cm

Distance from object

=100+100

=200 cm

q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : An electron microscope can achieve better resolving power than an optical microscope.

Reason R : The de Broglie's wavelength of the electrons emitted from an electron gun is much less than wavelength of visible light.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : An electron microscope can achieve better resolving power than an optical microscope.

Reason R : The de Broglie's wavelength of the electrons emitted from an electron gun is much less than wavelength of visible light.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false.
solution: Resolving power ∝1λ. Since the wavelength of electrons is much less than visible light, it's resolving power will be much more.

subject: Physics
q:
Consider following statements for refraction of light through prism, when angle of deviation is minimum.
(A) The refracted ray inside prism becomes parallel to the base.
(B) Larger angle prisms provide smaller angle of minimum deviation.
(C) Angle of incidence and angle of emergence becomes equal.
(D) There are always two sets of angle of incidence for which deviation will be same except at minimum deviation setting.
(E) Angle of refraction becomes double of prism angle.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Consider following statements for refraction of light through prism, when angle of deviation is minimum.
(A) The refracted ray inside prism becomes parallel to the base.
(B) Larger angle prisms provide smaller angle of minimum deviation.
(C) Angle of incidence and angle of emergence becomes equal.
(D) There are always two sets of angle of incidence for which deviation will be same except at minimum deviation setting.
(E) Angle of refraction becomes double of prism angle.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
$\delta=\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{e}-\mathrm{A}$
For $\delta_{\text {min }} \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{e}$ and refracted ray is parallel to base $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$ are correct

q: Two plane mirrors M1 and M2 are at right angle to each other shown. A point source P is placed at a and 2a meter away from M1 and M2 respectively. The shortest distance between the images thus formed is : (Take 5=2.3)
question: Two plane mirrors M1 and M2 are at right angle to each other shown. A point source P is placed at a and 2a meter away from M1 and M2 respectively. The shortest distance between the images thus formed is : (Take 5=2.3)
solution: We know that the formula of the distance between two points is, d=x2-x12+y2-y12, now put the values from the above the diagram in the above formula, then the distance between I1 and I2, d=(4a)2+(2a)2≈4.6a

subject: Physics
q: A mirror is used to produce an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{4}$. If the distance between object and its image is 40 cm , then the focal length of the mirror is ________
question: A mirror is used to produce an image with magnification of $\frac{1}{4}$. If the distance between object and its image is 40 cm , then the focal length of the mirror is ________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{-\mathrm{u}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}} \\ & \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=4 \mathrm{v} \\ & \mathrm{v}+\mathrm{u}=40 \\ & 5 \mathrm{v}=40 \\ & \mathrm{v}=8 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \mathrm{u}=32 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{8}-\frac{1}{32}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{4-1}{32}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \mathrm{f}=\frac{32}{3}=10.7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

q: The graph between angle of deviation <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>δ</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> and angle of incidence <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>i</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> for a triangular prism is represented by :
question: The graph between angle of deviation <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>δ</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> and angle of incidence <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>i</mi> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> for a triangular prism is represented by :
solution:
We know that angle of deviation depend upon the angle of incidence. If we determine experimentally the angle of deviation corresponding to different angles of incidence then and then plot i (on x-axis) and δ (on y-axis), we get a curve as shown in figure.

Clearly, if angle of incidence is gradually increased from a small value, the angle of deviation first decreases, becomes minimum for a particular angle of incidence and then begins to increase.

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement-1: Short wave transmission is achieved due to the total internal reflection of the e-m wave from an appropriate height in the ionosphere.
Statement-2: Refractive index of a plasma is independent of the frequency of e-m waves.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement-1: Short wave transmission is achieved due to the total internal reflection of the e-m wave from an appropriate height in the ionosphere.
Statement-2: Refractive index of a plasma is independent of the frequency of e-m waves.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is true but Statement $-2$ is not the correct explanation of statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true and Statement $-2$ is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
solution:
Effective refractive index of the ionosphere
$$
\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{eff}}=\mathrm{n}_0\left[1-\frac{80.5 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{f}^2}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$
where $\mathrm{f}$ is the frequency of em waves
